what_i_noticed: The signal engine composites 7 factors (crash_prob, regime, valuation, momentum, mean_reversion, external, macro_risk) but has zero drawdown awareness. It only sees 1M/3M/YTD returns, which measure direction but not distance from peak. A market that rallied +20% then dropped -10% shows +10% 3M return (bullish momentum signal) but is in a -10% drawdown (correction territory). The engine would call this 'Buy' when caution is warranted. Additionally, portfolio CVaR is daily-only (misleadingly small), and the R&D data generator gets null crash/signal fields because it bypasses the router layer.
hypothesis: Adding drawdown from 52-week high as an 8th signal component would improve signal quality by distinguishing between healthy trends and markets in correction/bear territory, especially in scenarios where returns mask underlying peak-to-trough pain.
what_i_did: 1. Added drawdown_pct parameter to get_market_signal() in signal_engine.py. 2. Implemented stepped signal mapping: near-highs (+0.2), pullback (0.0), correction (-0.3), bear (-0.7), crisis (-0.9). 3. Moved thresholds and signal values to config.py for tunability. 4. Extracted compute_drawdown_pct() utility function to eliminate code duplication between stock.py and market.py routers. 5. Updated both routers to compute S&P 500 drawdown and pass it to the signal engine. 6. Exposed drawdown_pct in market signal API response. 7. Rebalanced all 8 signal weights to sum to 1.0 (each reduced ~10% proportionally). 8. Wrote 37 tests covering signal behavior, edge cases, utility function, boundary conditions, and config validation.
analysis: The implementation is solid technically: 37 tests pass including all boundary conditions and edge cases, config is the single source of truth, and the utility function eliminates duplication. However, I must be honest about what this DOESN'T prove: I haven't validated that adding drawdown actually improves signal quality on historical data. The stepped thresholds (-2/-5/-10/-20) are based on conventional market taxonomy (pullback/correction/bear), not backtested. The 10% weight allocation is a judgment call, not optimized. In the current Bull regime with S&P near highs, this change has minimal impact on live signals (drawdown component will be +0.2, slightly boosting Buy signals). The real value shows up during corrections — and we can't test that without a walk-forward backtest through 2020/2022 drawdown periods. The feature is production-ready but unvalidated against historical outcomes.
what_i_would_do_differently: 1. Should have run a quick backtest through COVID (Mar 2020, -34% drawdown) and 2022 bear market (-25%) to validate the drawdown signal actually improved signal timing at those inflection points. 2. The stepped mapping is crude — a continuous function like np.clip(dd * 0.045, -0.9, 0.2) would be smoother and have fewer arbitrary thresholds, though less interpretable. 3. Could have also added per-stock drawdown (each stock's distance from its own 52W high) to get_stock_signal(), not just market-level. That would differentiate stocks more in sector rotations. 4. The 10% weight was chosen by feel (reduce each existing weight ~10%). A principled approach would use information ratio or out-of-sample signal contribution analysis.
next_steps: Backtest drawdown signal through 2020 COVID crash and 2022 bear market using walk_forward.py
Add per-stock drawdown to get_stock_signal() (stock's own 52W high, not market)
Implement Finding 1: annualized CVaR for portfolio analysis
Consider continuous drawdown mapping instead of stepped thresholds
Add drawdown visualization to frontend dashboard (drawdown from peak chart)
